1534	\begin{lemma}
1535	
1536	\leanok
1537	[Overlap compatibility of the tautological quotient]
1538	  \label{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}
1539	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient_overlap}
1540	  \uses{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback,
1541	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp, def:gr_bundleTransition, def:gr_chart_quotient,
1542	    def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap,
1543	    lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport,
1544	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}
1545	  For every pair of size-\(d\) subsets \(I, J\), the chart quotients \(u^I\)
1546	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) satisfy the overlap-compatibility condition required by the
1547	  section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom} for the bundle gluing \((g_{I,J})\):
1548	  the pullback-level identity \(g_{I,J} \circ f_{IJ}^{*} u^I = (t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*} u^J\)
1549	  of \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose} holds on each overlap \(U^I_J\).
1550	\end{lemma}
1551	\begin{proof}
1552	  \uses{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback,
1553	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp, def:gr_bundleTransition,
1554	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq,
1555	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}
1556	  By \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose} it suffices to prove the
1557	  pullback-level identity \(g_{I,J} \circ f_{IJ}^{*} u^I = (t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*} u^J\) on
1558	  \(U^I_J\). Both sides are morphisms of trivial bundles \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,r} \to
1559	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\); write each as \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) of a \(d \times r\)
1560	  matrix over the overlap ring \(R^I_J\) and compare matrices.
1561	
1562	  Since \(u^I = \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(X^I)\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
1563	  base-change naturality \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback} identifies \(f_{IJ}^{*} u^I\),
1564	  through the free-pullback comparisons, with \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) of the localised
1565	  matrix \(X^I\) over \(R^I_J\) (the global-sections base change is the away-localisation
1566	  \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}). The bundle transition \(g_{I,J}\) is
1567	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\) of the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)
1568	  (\cref{def:gr_bundleTransition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}); hence by the
1569	  square-after-rectangular composition law \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp},
1570	  \[
1571	    g_{I,J} \circ f_{IJ}^{*} u^I
1572	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEndRect}\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} \cdot X^I\bigr).
1573	  \]
1574	  The transition image matrix identity \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
1575	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}, transported across the overlap by
1576	  \cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}) turns the right-hand matrix into \(X^J\). Symmetrically,
1577	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}_J\) of \(X^J\) is, by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback} along
1578	  \(t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI}\), the comparison form of \((t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*} u^J\). Therefore
1579	  the two sides agree, which is the required overlap compatibility.
1580	\end{proof}
1581	
1582	\begin{definition}
1583	
1584	\leanok
1585	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
1586	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
1587	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
1588	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
1589	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
1590	    def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
1591	    def:gr_modules_glueHom, lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}
1592	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1593	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1594	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
1595	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1596	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
1597	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
1598	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
1599	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1600	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1601	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
1602	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
1603	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
1604	  \[
1605	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
1606	  \]
1607	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
1608	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). At the
1609	  module level this overlap compatibility -- the equalising condition consumed by the
1610	  section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom} -- is exactly
1611	  \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}, whose proof routes the matrix identity above
1612	  through the rectangular pullback/composition API
1613	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}) and the
1614	  adjunction transpose \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}.
1615	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
1616	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
1617	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
1618	  \[
1619	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1620	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
1621	  \]
1622	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
1623	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1624	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1625	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1626	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1627	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
1628	\end{definition}
1629	
1630	\section{The functor of points}
1631	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1632	
1633	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1634	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1635	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1636	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1637	identities that make that action functorial.
1638	
1639	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1640	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1642	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1643	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1644	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1645	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1646	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1647	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1648	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1649	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1650	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1651	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1652	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1653	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1654	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1655	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1656	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1657	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1658	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1659	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1660	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1661	  functor's value at \(T\).
1662	
1663	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1664	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1665	  \[
1666	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1667	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1668	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1669	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1670	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1671	  \]
1672	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1673	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1674	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1675	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1676	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1677	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1678	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1679	  map of equivalence classes.
1680	\end{definition}
1681	
1682	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
1683	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
1684	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
1685	
1686	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1687	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1688	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1689	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1690	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1691	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
1692	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
1693	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
1694	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
1695	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1696	\end{lemma}
1697	
1698	\begin{proof}
1699	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1700	  \leanok
1701	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
1702	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
1703	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
1704	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
1705	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
1706	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
1707	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
1708	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
1709	\end{proof}
1710	
1711	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1712	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
1713	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
1714	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
1715	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1716	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
1717	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
1718	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
1719	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1720	\end{lemma}
1721	
1722	\begin{proof}
1723	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1724	  \leanok
1725	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
1726	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
1727	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
1728	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
1729	\end{proof}
1730	
1731	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1732	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1733	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1735	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1736	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
1737	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
1738	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
1739	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
1740	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
1741	\end{lemma}
1742	
1743	\begin{proof}
1744	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
1745	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1746	    \leanok
1747	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
1748	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
1749	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
1750	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
1751	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
1752	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
1753	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
1754	  well-defined.
1755	\end{proof}
1756	
1757	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1758	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
1759	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1760	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1761	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
1762	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
1763	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
1764	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
1765	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
1766	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
1767	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
1768	\end{lemma}
1769	
1770	\begin{proof}
1771	
1772	\leanok
1773	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
1774	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
1775	  hom-set bijections.
1776	\end{proof}
1777	
1778	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1779	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1780	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1781	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1782	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1783	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
1784	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
1785	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and
1786	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
1787	\end{lemma}
1788	
1789	\begin{proof}
1790	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1791	  \leanok
1792	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
1793	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
1794	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf
1795	  tracked through each pullback).
1796	\end{proof}
1797	
1798	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1799	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
1800	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
1801	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
1802	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
1803	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
1804	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
1805	
1806	\begin{definition}
1807	
1808	\leanok
1809	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1810	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1811	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1812	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1813	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1814	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1815	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1816	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1817	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1818	  % "represents the
1819	  %  contravariant functor
1820	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1821	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1822	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1823	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1824	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1825	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1826	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1827	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1828	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1829	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1830	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1831	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1832	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1833	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1834	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1835	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1836	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1837	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1838	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1839	  \[
1840	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1841	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1842	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1843	  \]
1844	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1845	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1846	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1847	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1848	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1849	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1850	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1851	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1852	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1853	\end{definition}
1854	
1855	\section{The universal property}
1856	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1857	
1858	\begin{theorem}
1859	
1860	\leanok
1861	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1862	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1863	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1864	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1865	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1866	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1867	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1868	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1869	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1870	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1871	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1872	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1873	  %  contravariant functor
1874	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1875	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1876	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1877	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1878	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1879	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1880	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1881	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1882	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1883	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1884	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1885	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1886	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1887	  \[
1888	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1889	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1890	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1891	    \qquad
1892	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1893	      \;\longmapsto\;
1894	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1895	  \]
1896	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1897	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1898	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1899	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1900	  universal family.
1901	\end{theorem}
1902	
1903	\begin{proof}
1904	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1905	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1906	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1907	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1908	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1909	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1910	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1911	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1912	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1913	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1914	
1915	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1916	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1917	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1918	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1919	  the composite
1920	  \[
1921	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1922	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1923	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1924	  \]
1925	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1926	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1927	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1928	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1929	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1930	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1931	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1932	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1933	
1934	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1935	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1936	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1937	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1938	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1939	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1940	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1941	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1942	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1943	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1944	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1945	
1946	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1947	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1948	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1949	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1950	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1951	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1952	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1953	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
1954	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
1955	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
1956	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
1957	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
1958	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
1959	
1960	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
1961	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
1962	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
1963	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
1964	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
1965	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
1966	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
1967	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
1968	
1969	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
1970	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
1971	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1972	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1973	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57	
58	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
59	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
60	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
61	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
62	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
63	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
64	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
65	% there exists a finite filtration
66	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
67	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
68	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
69	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
70	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
74	\begin{proof}
75	
76	
77	\leanok

84	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62

5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;

51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at

41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor

5590	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
5591	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
5592	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
5593	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
5594	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
5595	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
5596	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
5597	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
5598	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
5599	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued

5604	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
5605	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
5606	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
5607	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
5608	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
5609	
5610	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
5611	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
5612	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The

5619	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified

- **Incomplete**: `Picard_GlueDescent.tex` MISSING (no file — critical); `Picard_GrassmannianQuot.tex` (4 Nitsure §5 blocks absent); `Picard_SectionGradedRing.tex` (5 SNAP targets not leanok).

- **Forward decls unresolved**: `Picard_GrassmannianQuot.tex` `lem:gr_modules_glue_unique` / `def:gr_modules_glueHom` — `\lean{}` targets (`Scheme.Modules.glue_unique`, `Scheme.Modules.glueHom`) not yet in Lean; marked "planned work", NOT leanok — prover needed.
- **Deps/Isolated**: `Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex` — 4 mathlibok anchors (`lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves`, `lem:mathlib_flat_of_localized_maximal`, `lem:mathlib_free_of_isLocalizedModule`, `lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars`) used only in proof-level `\uses{}`, isolated from DAG → **wire-up**: add to statement-level `\uses{}` of their consuming blocks.
- **DAG**: 0 `unknown_uses` (all `\uses{}` refs valid). 118 `unmatched_lean` — majority are expected `\mathlibok` anchors (Mathlib not in Lean scan) plus the 2 forward-decl project targets above.
- **Rendering**: blueprint-doctor clean — 0 malformed_refs, 0 broken_refs, 0 orphan chapters.

## Unstarted-phase proposals

### Proposed: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_GlueDescent.tex`
- **Covers**: `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GlueDescent.lean` | **Phase**: GR-quot effective-descent lane (GlueDescent infra for `Picard_GrassmannianQuot.lean`'s tautological quotient construction)
- **Why now**: `GlueDescent.lean` contains 2 sorries (in `isIso_glueRestrictionHom` and a helper); neither has a proof sketch → prover cannot be dispatched at all without this chapter.
- **Key declarations** (dependency order):

  2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}` — iso β_ij: the pullback `ι_i^* (ι_j)_* M_j ≅ f_{ij}^* M_j` (base-change iso between pushforwards on overlaps). `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}` [actual — in GlueDescent.lean L1148]. Source: GlueDescent.lean + `lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib` mathlibok anchor.
  3. `\lemma` `\label{lem:glueRestrictionHom}` — restriction hom `ι_i^* (glue D M g) ⟶ M i`. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionHom}` [actual — GlueDescent.lean L1042]. Source: equalizer universal property.
  4. `\theorem` `\label{thm:isIso_glueRestrictionHom}` — effective descent: `glueRestrictionHom` is an iso (sorry at GlueDescent.lean L1207). `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_glueRestrictionHom}` [actual].   5. `\definition` `\label{def:glueRestrictionIso}` — packaged iso form. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionIso}` [actual — GlueDescent.lean L1214]. Depends on `thm:isIso_glueRestrictionHom`.

The restriction hom `glueRestrictionHom i` extracts the i-th component via the equalizer projection `glueProj`.
